RS.MA-01.title: Incident Response Plan Execution
RS.MA-01.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization executes its incident response plan in a coordinated manner with relevant third parties once an incident is declared, including evidence of designated incident leads and integration with business continuity and disaster recovery plans.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan (IRP)
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates the existence of a formally approved, current incident response plan that defines roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and coordination with third parties.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan is version-controlled with review dates within the last 12 months
Named incident lead roles with alternates are clearly defined
Third-party coordination procedures (MSSP, legal counsel, law enforcement) are explicitly documented
Integration points with BCP and DR plans are cross-referenced
commonGaps: Plan has not been reviewed or updated in over 12 months
Third-party contact information is outdated or missing
No designated incident lead role or succession plan
Plan exists in draft form only without formal approval signatures
suggestedSources: CISO or IR team document repository

evidenceItems.name: Incident Declaration and Activation Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that incidents are formally declared and the IRP is activated with documented timestamps and responsible parties.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each incident has a formal declaration record with timestamp and declaring authority
Incident lead assignment is documented for every declared incident
Activation of supporting plans (BCP/DR) is recorded when triggered
Automated detection-to-declaration workflows are evidenced in SOAR platform
commonGaps: Incidents are handled informally without formal declaration
No consistent record of who declared the incident or when
Supporting plan activations are not tracked alongside the IR case
Gap between detection and declaration is not measured or tracked
suggestedSources: SOAR platform (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR, Tines)
IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Coordination Logs
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization coordinates with external parties such as MSSPs, legal counsel, law enforcement, or ISACs during incident response.
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication logs with third-party responders are timestamped and preserved
Retainer agreements with IR service providers are current and accessible
Evidence of tabletop exercises involving third-party participants
Clear handoff procedures between internal and external responders
commonGaps: No documented evidence of third-party engagement during incidents
IR retainer agreements are expired or nonexistent
Third parties are engaged ad hoc without following documented procedures
No after-action record of third-party contributions to incident response
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Tabletop and Simulation Exercise Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the IRP is tested through exercises to validate coordination procedures and readiness.
whatGoodLooksLike: At least one tabletop exercise conducted per year with documented results
Exercise scenarios reflect realistic and current threat landscape
Findings and corrective actions are tracked to closure
Third-party responders participate in exercises
commonGaps: No exercises conducted in the past 12 months
Exercises are superficial and do not test coordination with third parties
Findings from exercises are not tracked or remediated
Exercise scenarios are outdated or do not reflect current threats
suggestedSources: IR team shared drive or document repository

assessmentTips: Request the last three incident declaration records to verify the IRP was formally activated each time
Verify third-party contact lists in the IRP are current by cross-referencing with active contracts
Ask for evidence of the most recent tabletop exercise and confirm findings were remediated
Check that automated detection-to-declaration workflows exist in the SOAR platform
Confirm that BCP/DR plan activation criteria are cross-referenced in the IRP

RS.MA-02.title: Incident Triage and Validation
RS.MA-02.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that reported incidents undergo a structured triage process to confirm they are cybersecurity-related, validate their legitimacy, and estimate initial severity before committing response resources.

evidenceItems.name: Triage Procedures and Severity Classification Matrix
evidenceItems.description: Documents the criteria and workflow used to triage incoming incident reports and assign initial severity ratings.
whatGoodLooksLike: Severity classification matrix with clearly defined levels (e.g., P1-P4) and quantifiable criteria
Decision tree or flowchart for triage analysts to follow
Integration of threat intelligence feeds into triage decision-making
Defined SLAs for triage completion based on initial severity indicators
commonGaps: Severity levels are subjective with no defined criteria
No documented triage procedure exists; analysts rely on experience alone
Triage SLAs are undefined or not measured
No distinction between cybersecurity incidents and IT operational issues
suggestedSources: IR playbook repository (Confluence, wiki, or SOAR platform)

evidenceItems.name: Triage Activity Logs from SIEM/SOAR
evidenceItems.description: Shows actual triage decisions made on incoming alerts and incident reports, including analyst notes, validation outcomes, and severity assignments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each alert or report has a documented triage decision with analyst justification
False positives are tracked and used to tune detection rules
Triage timestamps show compliance with defined SLAs
Validation steps are recorded including evidence reviewed
commonGaps: Alerts are closed without documented triage rationale
No tracking of false positive rates to improve detection fidelity
Triage decisions lack timestamps making SLA compliance unmeasurable
Bulk closure of alerts without individual review
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security)
SOAR platform (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Validation Checklists
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that analysts follow a structured checklist to validate whether a reported event constitutes a genuine cybersecurity incident.
whatGoodLooksLike: Standardized checklist used consistently across all triage activities
Checklist includes verification of IOCs, affected assets, and data exposure
Completed checklists are attached to incident records
Checklists are reviewed and updated based on lessons learned
commonGaps: No standardized validation checklist exists
Checklists exist but are not consistently used or attached to incident records
Validation steps vary significantly between analysts
Checklists have not been updated to reflect current threat landscape
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

assessmentTips: Sample 5-10 recent incident records and verify each has a documented triage decision with severity assignment
Ask triage analysts to walk through their process for a recent alert to validate adherence to documented procedures
Review false positive tracking metrics to assess detection tuning maturity
Verify that triage SLAs are defined, measured, and reported to management
Check that the severity classification matrix aligns with the organization's risk appetite and BIA

RS.MA-03.title: Incident Categorization and Prioritization
RS.MA-03.assessorFocus: The assessor evaluates whether the organization categorizes incidents by type and prioritizes them based on scope, likely impact, and urgency, including balancing rapid recovery needs against investigative requirements.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Categorization Taxonomy
evidenceItems.description: Defines the organization's incident classification scheme including categories such as data breach, ransomware, DDoS, insider threat, and account compromise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Taxonomy aligns with industry standards (e.g., VERIS, US-CERT categories)
Each category has defined response strategies and playbooks
Taxonomy is integrated into ticketing and SOAR systems as mandatory fields
Regular reviews ensure new threat types are added to the taxonomy
commonGaps: No formal taxonomy; analysts use free-text categorization
Taxonomy exists but is not enforced in incident tracking tools
Categories are too broad to drive differentiated response strategies
Taxonomy has not been updated to include emerging threat types

evidenceItems.name: Prioritization Criteria and Decision Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that incidents are prioritized using defined criteria including business impact, scope of compromise, and time sensitivity.
whatGoodLooksLike: Prioritization matrix maps incident characteristics to response urgency levels
Business impact analysis (BIA) data is referenced in prioritization decisions
Documented justification for priority assignments in incident records
Re-prioritization events are logged when incident scope changes
commonGaps: Prioritization is based on analyst judgment without documented criteria
BIA data is not integrated into incident prioritization
No documentation of why a particular priority was assigned
Priority levels do not change even as incident scope evolves
suggestedSources: SOAR platform (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR)
IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Response Strategy Selection Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that for each incident, the team selects an appropriate response strategy balancing rapid recovery against investigative thoroughness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Decision records document the chosen strategy (contain-and-recover vs. monitor-and-investigate)
Strategy selection considers legal, regulatory, and evidence preservation requirements
Incident commander approval is documented for strategy decisions
Strategy changes during an incident are logged with justification
commonGaps: No documented strategy selection; response is reactive and unstructured
Legal and evidence preservation considerations are not factored in
No approval workflow for response strategy decisions
Strategy pivots during incidents are not recorded
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

assessmentTips: Review 5-10 recent incidents to verify categorization taxonomy was applied consistently
Ask how prioritization changes when an incident scope expands during investigation
Verify that the categorization taxonomy is enforced as a mandatory field in the ticketing system
Check that response strategy selection considers legal hold and evidence preservation
Confirm that BIA data informs prioritization decisions for incidents affecting critical systems

RS.MA-04.title: Incident Escalation and Elevation
RS.MA-04.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization has defined escalation criteria and procedures, tracks incident status in real time, and coordinates elevation to appropriate internal and external stakeholders when thresholds are met.

evidenceItems.name: Escalation Matrix and Criteria
evidenceItems.description: Defines the thresholds, timelines, and stakeholder notification requirements for escalating or elevating incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Escalation matrix maps severity levels to specific notification recipients and timelines
Criteria for elevation to executive leadership and board are clearly defined
Regulatory notification thresholds are embedded in the escalation matrix
Matrix is reviewed and updated at least annually
commonGaps: No formal escalation matrix; escalation decisions are ad hoc
Escalation timelines are undefined or unrealistic
Regulatory notification requirements are not integrated into escalation criteria
Matrix references roles that no longer exist or contact details that are outdated

evidenceItems.name: Incident Status Tracking Dashboard or Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that ongoing incidents are tracked in real time with status updates visible to relevant stakeholders.
whatGoodLooksLike: Real-time incident dashboard accessible to authorized stakeholders
Status updates are logged at regular intervals during active incidents
Dashboard includes escalation status and stakeholder notification timestamps
Historical incident status data is preserved for post-incident review
commonGaps: No centralized tracking of active incidents
Status updates are communicated informally via email or chat without preservation
Stakeholders lack visibility into incident progress
Historical status data is not retained after incident closure

evidenceItems.name: Escalation and Notification Logs
evidenceItems.description: Provides an audit trail of escalation actions taken during incidents, including timestamps and recipients.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every escalation event is timestamped and logged in the incident record
Notification receipts or acknowledgments from escalation recipients are captured
Automated escalation workflows trigger when SLA thresholds are breached
Escalation logs are part of the permanent incident record
commonGaps: Escalation actions are not logged or timestamped
No confirmation that stakeholders received and acknowledged escalation notifications
Automated escalation workflows do not exist; all escalation is manual
Escalation records are stored separately from incident records
suggestedSources: SOAR platform (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR)
PagerDuty, Opsgenie, or equivalent on-call management system

assessmentTips: Select a recent high-severity incident and trace the escalation path against the documented matrix
Verify that automated escalation triggers exist in the SOAR or ticketing platform for SLA breaches
Confirm that executive and board notification criteria are defined and have been tested
Review escalation logs for evidence that regulatory notification timelines were met
Ask how the organization handles escalation when the primary contact is unavailable

RS.MA-05.title: Incident Recovery Initiation Criteria
RS.MA-05.assessorFocus: The assessor checks whether the organization has defined criteria for transitioning from incident response to recovery, considering both the incident characteristics and the potential operational disruption of recovery activities.

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Initiation Criteria Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Defines the conditions under which incident recovery processes should be initiated, including readiness checks and risk considerations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Explicit criteria specify when containment is sufficiently complete to begin recovery
Criteria address both technical readiness and business impact considerations
Decision authority for initiating recovery is clearly assigned
Criteria are integrated into the IRP and referenced during incident handling
commonGaps: No documented criteria; recovery initiation is ad hoc
Criteria focus only on technical factors and ignore business impact
Decision authority for recovery initiation is unclear
Criteria exist in the plan but are not referenced during actual incidents

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Initiation Decision Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that for actual incidents, the decision to initiate recovery was made using the defined criteria with documented approval.
whatGoodLooksLike: Decision records reference specific criteria that were evaluated
Approval from designated authority is documented with timestamp
Potential operational disruption of recovery actions was considered and documented
Decision records are linked to the parent incident record
commonGaps: No documented decision to transition from response to recovery
Recovery begins without formal evaluation of readiness criteria
Operational disruption risk of recovery actions is not assessed
Decision records are not preserved as part of the incident documentation
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Impact Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the potential operational disruption of recovery activities is evaluated before initiating recovery.
whatGoodLooksLike: Impact assessments consider downtime required for restoration activities
Scheduling of recovery actions accounts for business-critical windows
Stakeholder input on acceptable recovery windows is documented
Contingency plans exist for recovery actions that may cause additional disruption
commonGaps: Recovery actions are initiated without assessing secondary disruption
Business-critical time windows are not considered in recovery scheduling
No stakeholder consultation before initiating recovery actions
Contingency plans for failed recovery attempts do not exist

assessmentTips: Ask the IR team to describe how they decide when to transition from response to recovery
Review a recent incident to verify the recovery initiation decision was documented against criteria
Confirm that operational disruption assessments are performed before recovery actions begin
Verify that recovery initiation criteria account for evidence preservation requirements
Check that business stakeholders are consulted on recovery timing for incidents affecting critical systems

RS.AN-01.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RS.MA-02
RS.AN-01.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into RS.MA-02. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under RS.MA-02 (Incident Triage and Validation).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RS.MA-02 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RS.MA-02.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.AN-01 controls to RS.MA-02
Triage and validation procedures cover original RS.AN-01 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.AN-01 in its control framework without mapping to RS.MA-02
Legacy procedures tied to RS.AN-01 have not been migrated
Control mapping documentation is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.AN-01 have been remapped to RS.MA-02
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RS.AN-02.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RS.MA-02, RS.MA-03, RS.MA-04
RS.AN-02.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and its scope distributed across RS.MA-02, RS.MA-03, and RS.MA-04. Assessors should evaluate relevant controls under those subcategories.

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RS.MA-02, RS.MA-03, RS.MA-04 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RS.MA-02 (Triage), RS.MA-03 (Categorization), and RS.MA-04 (Escalation).
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.AN-02 controls across RS.MA-02, RS.MA-03, and RS.MA-04
No control coverage gaps from the redistribution
Migration mapping is formally documented and approved
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.AN-02 without mapping to successor subcategories
Partial migration leaves control gaps
No documentation of how RS.AN-02 scope was redistributed

assessmentTips: Verify controls previously mapped to RS.AN-02 are redistributed across RS.MA-02, RS.MA-03, and RS.MA-04
Check that no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Confirm the organization's CSF mapping reflects the 2.0 structure

RS.AN-03.title: Incident Root Cause Analysis
RS.AN-03.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization performs thorough analysis during incidents to establish the sequence of events, identify involved assets, determine vulnerabilities exploited, and identify systemic root causes.

evidenceItems.name: Root Cause Analysis Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that completed incidents include a formal root cause analysis identifying the underlying systemic causes, not just proximate triggers.
whatGoodLooksLike: RCA reports use structured methodologies (5 Whys, Ishikawa, fault tree analysis)
Reports identify both technical vulnerabilities and process failures
Root causes are linked to remediation actions with owners and deadlines
RCA findings feed into the organization's risk register
commonGaps: Root cause analysis is superficial, stopping at the proximate cause
No structured RCA methodology is applied consistently
RCA findings do not result in tracked remediation actions
RCA is only performed for high-severity incidents, missing patterns in lower-severity events
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Timeline and Event Sequence Reconstruction
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization reconstructs the full sequence of events during an incident, including initial compromise, lateral movement, and data exfiltration stages.
whatGoodLooksLike: Detailed timelines with timestamps correlating events across multiple data sources
Timeline includes attacker TTPs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK framework
Timelines identify gaps in visibility that hindered analysis
Automated timeline reconstruction tools are used (e.g., log correlation in SIEM)
commonGaps: Timelines are incomplete due to insufficient logging
Events are not correlated across multiple data sources
No mapping to MITRE ATT&CK or equivalent framework
Timeline reconstruction is manual and inconsistent across incidents
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security)
EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability and Threat Actor Attribution Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Documents the vulnerabilities exploited and the threat actors or threat types involved in the incident.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exploited vulnerabilities are identified with CVE references where applicable
Threat actor attribution is attempted using available threat intelligence
Analysis correlates findings with threat intelligence feeds and ISAC advisories
Findings inform vulnerability management prioritization
commonGaps: Exploited vulnerabilities are not identified or documented
No attempt at threat actor attribution or TTP identification
Threat intelligence is not leveraged during incident analysis
Findings are not shared with vulnerability management for prioritization
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform (MISP, Recorded Future, Mandiant Advantage)

evidenceItems.name: Deception Technology Analysis Reports
evidenceItems.description: Shows that deception technologies (honeypots, honeytokens, decoy systems) are checked for additional intelligence on attacker behavior during incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Deception technology alerts are correlated with active incidents
Attacker interactions with decoys provide additional TTP intelligence
Deception findings are integrated into incident analysis reports
Deception coverage is mapped to critical assets and likely attack paths
commonGaps: No deception technology is deployed
Deception technology exists but is not checked during incident analysis
Deception alerts are not correlated with incident records
Deception coverage is limited and does not align with high-value targets

assessmentTips: Request RCA reports from the last three significant incidents and verify structured methodologies were applied
Check that incident timelines correlate events across SIEM, EDR, and network data sources
Verify that root causes are tracked to remediation with assigned owners and completion dates
Ask whether MITRE ATT&CK mapping is performed during incident analysis
Confirm that deception technology outputs are reviewed as part of incident investigation

RS.AN-04.title: Withdrawn - Moved to RS.MA-03
RS.AN-04.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and moved to RS.MA-03. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under RS.MA-03 (Incident Categorization and Prioritization).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RS.MA-03 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RS.MA-03.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.AN-04 controls to RS.MA-03
Categorization and prioritization procedures cover original RS.AN-04 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.AN-04 in its control framework without mapping to RS.MA-03
Legacy procedures tied to RS.AN-04 have not been migrated
Control mapping documentation is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.AN-04 have been remapped to RS.MA-03
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RS.AN-05.title: Withdrawn - Moved to ID.RA-08
RS.AN-05.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and moved to ID.RA-08 under the Identify function. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under ID.RA-08.

evidenceItems.name: Refer to ID.RA-08 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under ID.RA-08.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.AN-05 controls to ID.RA-08
Risk assessment procedures cover the original RS.AN-05 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.AN-05 without mapping to ID.RA-08
Legacy procedures tied to RS.AN-05 have not been migrated to the Identify function
Cross-function control mapping is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.AN-05 have been remapped to ID.RA-08
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal and cross-function move
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RS.AN-06.title: Investigation Action Recording and Integrity
RS.AN-06.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that all actions performed during an incident investigation are recorded with maintained integrity and provenance, including immutable audit trails of responder activities.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Responder Activity Logs
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that each incident responder records their actions during investigation and response, creating an immutable audit trail.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every responder action is timestamped and attributed to a named individual
Activity logs are immutable once recorded (write-once storage or cryptographic integrity)
Logs capture both automated and manual actions taken during response
Activity logging is mandatory per SOC/IR operating procedures
commonGaps: Responder actions are not systematically recorded
Activity logs can be edited or deleted after the fact
Only senior responders log their actions; junior analyst activities are undocumented
Automated actions by SOAR playbooks are not logged alongside manual actions
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Documentation with Integrity Controls
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the incident lead maintains comprehensive documentation with integrity protections throughout the investigation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Incident documentation is stored in a system with version control and audit trails
Document integrity is verifiable through checksums or digital signatures
Information sources cited in reports are traceable and preserved
Documentation templates ensure consistent and complete recording
commonGaps: Incident documentation is stored in shared drives without version control
No integrity verification mechanisms for incident records
Sources of information in reports are not cited or preserved
Documentation quality varies significantly between incident leads
suggestedSources: Document management system with version control (SharePoint, Confluence)
IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Investigation Action Recording Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documents the policy requiring all investigation actions to be recorded and the procedures for maintaining record integrity.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly requires immutable recording of all responder actions
Procedures specify the tools and methods for recording and preserving actions
Training records confirm responders are trained on documentation requirements
Compliance with recording requirements is periodically audited
commonGaps: No policy requiring documentation of investigation actions
Policy exists but procedures for ensuring immutability are not defined
Responders are not trained on documentation requirements
No periodic audit of compliance with recording requirements

assessmentTips: Select a recent incident and verify that all responder actions are recorded with timestamps and attribution
Test whether activity logs are truly immutable by checking for edit or delete capabilities
Verify that automated SOAR actions are logged alongside manual responder actions
Check that incident documentation includes integrity controls such as version history or checksums
Confirm that investigation recording requirements are included in IR team training

RS.AN-07.title: Incident Data and Metadata Collection and Integrity
RS.AN-07.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization collects, preserves, and safeguards the integrity of all pertinent incident data and metadata following evidence preservation and chain-of-custody procedures.

evidenceItems.name: Evidence Collection and Chain-of-Custody Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documents the procedures for collecting, preserving, and maintaining chain of custody for incident data and metadata.
whatGoodLooksLike: Chain-of-custody procedures define collection, handling, transfer, and storage requirements
Procedures address both digital and physical evidence preservation
Evidence handling aligns with legal admissibility requirements
Procedures specify metadata to be captured (source, date/time, collector, hash values)
commonGaps: No formal chain-of-custody procedures exist
Procedures do not address digital evidence preservation adequately
Legal admissibility requirements are not considered
Metadata requirements are not defined, leading to incomplete evidence records

evidenceItems.name: Evidence Collection Logs and Chain-of-Custody Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows actual evidence collection activities with maintained chain of custody for specific incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each evidence item has a chain-of-custody log with timestamps and custodian signatures
Cryptographic hashes are computed and recorded at collection time
Evidence storage locations are secure and access-controlled
Transfer of evidence between custodians is formally documented
commonGaps: No chain-of-custody documentation for collected evidence
Cryptographic hashes are not computed, making integrity unverifiable
Evidence is stored in unsecured or shared locations
Evidence transfers between parties are not documented
suggestedSources: IR case management tool evidence module

evidenceItems.name: Forensic Data Preservation and Storage Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that forensic images, log captures, memory dumps, and other incident artifacts are securely preserved with integrity verification.
whatGoodLooksLike: Forensic images are stored on write-once or integrity-protected media
Preservation actions are performed using validated forensic tools
Storage media and locations are inventoried and access-controlled
Retention periods align with legal hold requirements and organizational policy
commonGaps: Forensic images are stored without integrity protection
Non-forensic tools are used for evidence collection, risking data modification
No inventory of preserved evidence or its storage locations
Evidence is deleted prematurely without considering legal hold obligations

assessmentTips: Request chain-of-custody records from a recent incident and verify completeness
Verify that cryptographic hashes are computed at evidence collection time and verified periodically
Check that forensic tools used for evidence collection are validated and documented
Confirm that evidence storage locations are access-controlled with audit logging
Review legal hold procedures to ensure evidence is not destroyed prematurely

RS.AN-08.title: Incident Magnitude Estimation and Validation
RS.AN-08.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization systematically estimates and validates the magnitude of incidents by searching for indicators of compromise and evidence of persistence across potentially affected targets.

evidenceItems.name: Scope Assessment and IOC Sweep Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that during incidents the organization actively searches other potential targets for indicators of compromise to determine the true scope.
whatGoodLooksLike: IOC sweeps are performed across the entire environment, not just known-affected systems
Automated IOC scanning tools are deployed to accelerate scope assessment
Sweep results are documented with timestamps and coverage metrics
Newly discovered compromised systems are added to the incident scope
commonGaps: Scope assessment is limited to initially reported systems
No systematic IOC sweep across the broader environment
Automated scanning tools are not available or not used during incidents
Sweep coverage is not measured, leaving potential blind spots
suggestedSources: EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne)

evidenceItems.name: Persistence Mechanism Detection Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization checks for persistence mechanisms established by threat actors to validate whether the adversary has maintained ongoing access.
whatGoodLooksLike: Checks for common persistence techniques (scheduled tasks, registry keys, web shells, backdoors)
Persistence detection is mapped to MITRE ATT&CK Persistence tactics
Both automated and manual persistence checks are performed
Findings are documented and drive eradication activities
commonGaps: No systematic check for persistence mechanisms
Only basic persistence checks are performed, missing advanced techniques
Persistence detection is not mapped to a framework like MITRE ATT&CK
Findings are not documented or linked to eradication actions

evidenceItems.name: Incident Magnitude Validation and Reassessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the estimated magnitude of an incident is validated through analysis and reassessed as new information emerges.
whatGoodLooksLike: Initial magnitude estimates are documented with supporting evidence
Magnitude is reassessed at defined intervals or when new information emerges
Reassessments trigger updates to response strategy and resource allocation
Final validated magnitude is documented in the incident closure report
commonGaps: Initial magnitude estimates are not formally documented
No process for reassessing magnitude as the investigation progresses
Magnitude changes do not trigger response strategy adjustments
Final incident reports do not include validated magnitude assessment
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

assessmentTips: Ask how the organization determines whether additional systems are compromised beyond the initially identified targets
Review IOC sweep coverage to verify it extends beyond the immediately affected network segment
Check that persistence detection procedures reference MITRE ATT&CK Persistence tactics
Verify that magnitude reassessments are documented when new compromised systems are discovered
Confirm that automated IOC scanning capabilities exist and were used in recent incidents

RS.CO-01.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into PR.AT-01
RS.CO-01.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into PR.AT-01 under the Protect function. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under PR.AT-01 (Awareness and Training).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to PR.AT-01 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under PR.AT-01.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.CO-01 controls to PR.AT-01
Awareness and training procedures cover original RS.CO-01 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.CO-01 without mapping to PR.AT-01
Legacy procedures tied to RS.CO-01 have not been migrated to the Protect function
Cross-function control mapping is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.CO-01 have been remapped to PR.AT-01
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal and cross-function move
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RS.CO-02.title: Stakeholder Incident Notification
RS.CO-02.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization has defined procedures for notifying internal and external stakeholders of incidents, including breach notification to affected customers, business partners, law enforcement, and regulatory bodies.

evidenceItems.name: Breach Notification Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documents the organization's procedures for notifying affected parties after a data breach, including regulatory timelines and notification content requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures address all applicable breach notification laws and regulations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, state laws)
Notification timelines are clearly defined and align with regulatory requirements
Template notification letters are pre-approved by legal counsel
Procedures specify who authorizes the notification decision
commonGaps: Breach notification procedures do not cover all applicable jurisdictions
Notification timelines are not aligned with regulatory requirements
No pre-approved notification templates exist, causing delays during actual breaches
Authorization authority for breach notifications is unclear

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that during actual incidents, stakeholders were notified in accordance with documented procedures and within required timelines.
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification records include recipient, method, timestamp, and content
Notifications were sent within regulatory timelines where applicable
Customer, partner, and regulatory notifications are tracked separately
Acknowledgment of receipt is captured where feasible
commonGaps: No records of stakeholder notifications exist for past incidents
Notifications were sent late, exceeding regulatory timelines
Notification content was not pre-approved by legal counsel
No tracking of notification delivery or acknowledgment
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)
Email delivery logs and certified mail records

evidenceItems.name: Law Enforcement and Regulatory Body Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization notifies law enforcement and regulatory bodies when incident criteria are met, with documented approval from management.
whatGoodLooksLike: Criteria for law enforcement notification are defined in the IRP
Management approval for law enforcement engagement is documented
Regulatory body notifications include required data elements and are filed within deadlines
Follow-up communications with law enforcement are tracked
commonGaps: No criteria defined for when to notify law enforcement
Law enforcement notifications occur without management approval
Regulatory notifications are incomplete or filed late
No tracking of law enforcement engagement or case numbers

evidenceItems.name: Contractual Notification Obligations Register
evidenceItems.description: Documents contractual obligations with business partners and customers for incident notification, including timelines and required information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register of all contracts with incident notification clauses is maintained
Notification timelines per contract are mapped and accessible during incidents
Contractual obligations are integrated into the incident notification workflow
Register is reviewed and updated when contracts are renewed or modified
commonGaps: No centralized register of contractual notification obligations
Contractual notification timelines are not known by the IR team
Obligations are not integrated into incident response workflows
Register is outdated and does not reflect current contracts

assessmentTips: Request evidence of stakeholder notifications from the most recent data breach incident
Verify that breach notification procedures cover all applicable regulatory jurisdictions
Check that pre-approved notification templates exist and were used during actual incidents
Confirm that contractual notification obligations are known and accessible to the IR team
Review whether regulatory notification deadlines were met for past incidents

RS.CO-03.title: Incident Information Sharing
RS.CO-03.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization shares incident information with designated internal and external stakeholders in a secure, consistent manner aligned with response plans, information sharing agreements, and need-to-know principles.

evidenceItems.name: Information Sharing Agreements and Protocols
evidenceItems.description: Documents the agreements and protocols governing how incident information is shared with external parties such as ISACs, suppliers, and government agencies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current information sharing agreements are in place with relevant ISACs and sector partners
Protocols define what information can be shared, with whom, and through what channels
Data sanitization procedures ensure sensitive organizational data is removed before sharing
TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) or equivalent classification is applied to shared information
commonGaps: No information sharing agreements are in place
Sharing protocols do not define data sanitization requirements
No classification scheme (TLP) is applied to shared incident information
Information sharing is ad hoc without formal agreements
suggestedSources: ISAC membership records and sharing agreements

evidenceItems.name: Internal Stakeholder Communication Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that incident information is shared with appropriate internal stakeholders including senior leadership, HR, and business unit heads.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular status updates to senior leadership are documented during major incidents
HR is notified when insider threat activity is detected
Business unit heads are informed of incidents affecting their operations
Communication follows defined channels and frequency in the IRP
commonGaps: Senior leadership receives inconsistent or delayed incident updates
HR notification for insider incidents is informal or delayed
Business units are not informed of incidents affecting their systems
No defined communication cadence during active incidents
suggestedSources: IR case management tool communication logs
Incident bridge call recordings or minutes

evidenceItems.name: External Threat Intelligence Sharing Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization voluntarily shares threat intelligence (TTPs, IOCs) with sector partners and ISACs after sanitizing sensitive data.
whatGoodLooksLike: IOCs and TTPs are shared with ISACs in standardized formats (STIX/TAXII)
Shared intelligence has organizational identifiers and sensitive data removed
Sharing contributions are tracked and receipts maintained
Organization both contributes to and consumes from sharing communities
commonGaps: Organization consumes threat intelligence but does not contribute back
No standardized format for sharing (STIX/TAXII)
Sensitive organizational data is inadvertently included in shared intelligence
No tracking of what intelligence was shared and with whom
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform (MISP, Recorded Future, Anomali)

evidenceItems.name: Supplier Incident Communication Protocols
evidenceItems.description: Documents the rules and protocols for sharing incident information with critical suppliers and receiving incident reports from them.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contractual protocols for incident information exchange with suppliers are defined
Communication channels and escalation procedures with key suppliers are tested
Crisis communication methods with critical suppliers are documented
Supplier incident reports are integrated into the organization's incident tracking
commonGaps: No defined protocols for incident information exchange with suppliers
Communication channels with suppliers are not tested until an actual incident
Supplier-reported incidents are not integrated into organizational tracking
Crisis communication with suppliers relies on informal channels

assessmentTips: Request evidence of ISAC membership and recent intelligence sharing contributions
Verify that TLP or equivalent classification is applied to all shared incident information
Check that internal communication records show regular updates to leadership during major incidents
Confirm that data sanitization procedures are applied before sharing intelligence externally
Review supplier communication protocols and evidence they have been tested

RS.CO-04.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RS.MA-01, RS.MA-04
RS.CO-04.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and its scope distributed between RS.MA-01 (IRP Execution) and RS.MA-04 (Escalation). Assessors should evaluate relevant controls under those subcategories.

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RS.MA-01 and RS.MA-04 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RS.MA-01 and RS.MA-04.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.CO-04 controls to RS.MA-01 and RS.MA-04
Coordination procedures cover original RS.CO-04 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.CO-04 without mapping to successor subcategories
Partial migration leaves control gaps between RS.MA-01 and RS.MA-04
No documentation of how RS.CO-04 scope was redistributed

assessmentTips: Verify controls previously mapped to RS.CO-04 are redistributed between RS.MA-01 and RS.MA-04
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Confirm the organization's CSF mapping reflects the 2.0 structure

RS.CO-05.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RS.CO-03
RS.CO-05.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into RS.CO-03. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under RS.CO-03 (Incident Information Sharing).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RS.CO-03 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RS.CO-03.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.CO-05 controls to RS.CO-03
Information sharing procedures cover original RS.CO-05 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.CO-05 without mapping to RS.CO-03
Legacy procedures tied to RS.CO-05 have not been migrated
Control mapping documentation is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.CO-05 have been remapped to RS.CO-03
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RS.MI-01.title: Incident Containment
RS.MI-01.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization has both automated and manual capabilities to contain incidents, including network isolation, endpoint quarantine, and account disablement, and that containment actions are executed promptly and documented.

evidenceItems.name: Containment Playbooks and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documents the predefined containment strategies and playbooks for different incident types, including automated and manual containment actions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Containment playbooks exist for each incident category (ransomware, data breach, DDoS, etc.)
Playbooks specify both automated responses and manual fallback procedures
Containment actions include network isolation, endpoint quarantine, and account lockout
Playbooks define authorization requirements for disruptive containment actions
commonGaps: No predefined containment playbooks; containment is improvised during incidents
Playbooks only cover a limited set of incident types
Automated containment capabilities are not configured or tested
Authorization requirements for containment actions are unclear

evidenceItems.name: Automated Containment Action Logs
evidenceItems.description: Shows that automated containment actions were triggered by security technologies during actual incidents, including the specific actions taken and their outcomes.
whatGoodLooksLike: EDR, firewall, and SOAR logs show automated containment actions with timestamps
Automated actions include endpoint isolation, malicious process termination, and network block rules
Outcomes of automated containment are logged (success/failure)
Automated actions trigger notifications to the IR team for review
commonGaps: No automated containment capabilities are deployed
Automated actions are not logged or logged without sufficient detail
Automated containment is configured but never tested
No notification to IR team when automated containment occurs
suggestedSources: EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne)
SOAR platform (Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR)
Firewall and network security appliance logs

evidenceItems.name: Manual Containment Action Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that incident responders select and perform manual containment actions when automated responses are insufficient or inappropriate.
whatGoodLooksLike: Manual containment actions are documented in the incident record with justification
Responders record the rationale for choosing specific containment strategies
Containment actions are authorized by the incident commander before execution
Impact of containment actions on business operations is assessed and documented
commonGaps: Manual containment actions are not documented in incident records
No authorization workflow for manual containment decisions
Impact of containment on business operations is not assessed before execution
Containment actions are taken without coordination among responders
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Containment Coordination Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that third parties (ISPs, MSSPs) perform containment actions on behalf of the organization when needed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Service agreements with MSSPs define containment capabilities and authorization procedures
ISP takedown requests are documented with timestamps and outcomes
Third-party containment actions are integrated into the incident record
Coordination procedures with third-party responders are tested periodically
commonGaps: No agreements with third parties for containment support
Third-party containment actions are not tracked in the incident record
ISP takedown request procedures are not documented
Third-party containment capabilities have not been tested

assessmentTips: Request containment playbooks for the top three incident types and verify they include both automated and manual actions
Review EDR and SOAR logs from a recent incident to verify automated containment was triggered
Confirm that remediation VLAN or network isolation capabilities are tested and functional
Check that containment actions require incident commander authorization for high-impact actions
Verify that third-party containment capabilities are defined in MSSP agreements and tested

RS.MI-02.title: Incident Eradication
RS.MI-02.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization has capabilities and procedures to eradicate threats from the environment, including removal of malware, closure of unauthorized access, and elimination of persistence mechanisms.

evidenceItems.name: Eradication Procedures and Playbooks
evidenceItems.description: Documents the procedures for eradicating threats including malware removal, backdoor elimination, credential reset, and vulnerability remediation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Eradication playbooks address common threat types (malware, ransomware, unauthorized access)
Procedures include verification steps to confirm successful eradication
Credential reset and access revocation procedures are defined for compromised accounts
Eradication procedures address underlying vulnerabilities, not just symptoms
commonGaps: No formal eradication procedures; actions are improvised
Eradication focuses on symptoms without addressing root vulnerabilities
No verification steps to confirm threats are fully eradicated
Credential reset procedures are not comprehensive (e.g., service accounts missed)

evidenceItems.name: Eradication Action Logs
evidenceItems.description: Shows the specific eradication actions taken during actual incidents, including automated and manual remediation steps and their outcomes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each eradication action is timestamped, attributed, and documented in the incident record
Automated eradication actions from EDR and antivirus are logged
Manual actions such as system reimaging and credential resets are documented
Verification scans confirm successful eradication
commonGaps: Eradication actions are performed but not documented
No verification that eradication was successful before returning systems to production
Automated and manual eradication actions are not consolidated in the incident record
Partial eradication occurs, leaving some persistence mechanisms intact
suggestedSources: EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)

evidenceItems.name: Post-Eradication Verification Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that the organization verifies the completeness of eradication before transitioning to recovery.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-eradication scans are performed using updated signatures and IOC lists
Verification includes checking for all known persistence mechanisms
Clean state is confirmed before systems are cleared for recovery
Verification results are documented and signed off by the incident lead
commonGaps: No post-eradication verification is performed
Verification scans use outdated signatures or incomplete IOC lists
Systems are returned to production without confirming clean state
Verification results are not documented or reviewed

assessmentTips: Review eradication records from a recent incident to verify all threat components were removed
Confirm that post-eradication verification scans are performed before recovery begins
Check that credential reset procedures cover all affected account types including service accounts
Verify that eradication addresses root cause vulnerabilities, not just malware artifacts
Ask about third-party MSSP eradication capabilities and how they are coordinated

RS.MI-03.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into ID.RA-06
RS.MI-03.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into ID.RA-06 under the Identify function. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under ID.RA-06.

evidenceItems.name: Refer to ID.RA-06 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under ID.RA-06.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RS.MI-03 controls to ID.RA-06
Vulnerability management procedures cover original RS.MI-03 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RS.MI-03 without mapping to ID.RA-06
Legacy procedures tied to RS.MI-03 have not been migrated to the Identify function
Cross-function control mapping is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RS.MI-03 have been remapped to ID.RA-06
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal and cross-function move
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation
